steps.dialogue: Welcome back. It's great to see you. I've been looking forward to continuing our fraction work together.

Last lesson, you learned that different fractions can equal the same amount: [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] equals [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] equals [fraction numerator=3 denominator=6]three sixths[/fraction]. You built your knowledge by comparing shaded amounts in bars AND by checking that they land at the same point on the number line. Two ways to show they're equal.
Now we'll compare fractions that aren't equivalent - or equal - to find which is larger or smaller.

Keep in mind what we worked on last lesson. If [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] equals [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction], then [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] is more than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].
